**Sheffield Arena**, known for sponsorship purposes as **Utilita Arena Sheffield**, is a multi-purpose arena located in [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield"), England. It is situated near [Meadowhall](/wiki/Meadowhall_Centre "Meadowhall Centre") and lies between [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield") city centre and [Rotherham](/wiki/Rotherham "Rotherham") town centre.

Opened in 1991, it is used for concerts and sporting events, and is also home to the [Sheffield Steelers](/wiki/Sheffield_Steelers "Sheffield Steelers") [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") club. Attendance for all events at the venue has totalled around 14 million since its opening. It has a maximum capacity of 13,600.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/1993_Sheffield_Arena_queue_1_%2814877204155%29.jpg/250px-1993_Sheffield_Arena_queue_1_%2814877204155%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:1993_Sheffield_Arena_queue_1_(14877204155).jpg)

Spectators queuing outside the arena in 1993

Constructed at a cost of [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")34 million, it was opened by [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") on 30 May 1991 as Sheffield Arena.[[8]](#cite_note-about-8) The first concert took place that evening, [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon") playing as part of his "Born at the Right Time" tour. The arena then took on the role of Gymnastics Hall for the [1991 World Student Games](/wiki/1991_Summer_Universiade "1991 Summer Universiade") which were held in the city and which prompted the investment in, and development of, both the arena and nearby [Don Valley Stadium](/wiki/Don_Valley_Stadium "Don Valley Stadium").

Since the venue opened in 1991 the arena has averaged 122 shows a year with over 650,000 customers passing through the doors each year.[[9]](#cite_note-auto-9)

The Arena was the location of the infamous [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") [Sheffield Rally](/wiki/Sheffield_Rally "Sheffield Rally") during the [1992 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/1992_United_Kingdom_general_election "1992 United Kingdom general election") which was widely criticised for being prematurely triumphalist.[[10]](#cite_note-McDonald-10)

The arena was renamed [Hallam FM](/wiki/Hallam_FM "Hallam FM") Arena in 2002, but returned to its original name from 1 November 2007.[[11]](#cite_note-11) On 9 August 2010, Sheffield Arena was officially renamed Motorpoint Arena Sheffield, after the [Derby](/wiki/Derby "Derby") based car sales company invested £1 million to rename the venue.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In August 2015 the arena was once again rebranded as Sheffield Arena, however in September 2017 nearby [Doncaster Sheffield Airport](/wiki/Doncaster_Sheffield_Airport "Doncaster Sheffield Airport") began sponsoring the arena giving it a rebrand, FlyDSA Arena.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2021, [Utilita Energy](/wiki/Utilita_Energy "Utilita Energy") took over sponsorship of the arena.

The arena underwent a major renovation installing a new roof, doors, seats, lighting, catering and toilet facilities in July 2013.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 2013 the arena was ranked as the 18th most popular arena in the world and this was in the year that the building was closed for over 2 months to enable the multi-million pound refurbishment of the venue. More recently, in 2019, [Pollstar](/wiki/Pollstar "Pollstar") ranked the arena at 65th worldwide and 7th in the UK as part of their 'Top 200 Arena Venues'
with 311,125 tickets sold,[[15]](#cite_note-15) up 5 places from 70th in 2018.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### COVID-19 pandemic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: COVID-19 pandemic")]

During the [COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom"), the car park of the arena was used as a drive-through [phlebotomy](/wiki/Phlebotomy "Phlebotomy") service operated by [Sheffield Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust](/wiki/Sheffield_Teaching_Hospitals_NHS_Foundation_Trust "Sheffield Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust"), the first of its kind in the country. This allowed patients to attend for routine blood tests from within the isolation of their cars, reducing the requirement for patients to attend [GPs](/wiki/General_practitioner "General practitioner") or hospital clinics. The drive-through phlebotomy service opened on 8 April 2020 for patients with underlying health conditions,[[17]](#cite_note-17) and was expanded to become available to all patients in the Sheffield area on 27 April.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The drive-through was deemed such a success that it was turned into a permanent service, relocating first to a warehouse next to the [Sheffield Parkway](/wiki/Sheffield_Parkway "Sheffield Parkway") before settling into a permanent site next to the [Northern General Hospital](/wiki/Northern_General_Hospital "Northern General Hospital") after the end of the pandemic.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

To support the rollout of the local COVID-19 [vaccination campaign](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccination_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 vaccination in the United Kingdom"), the Sheffield NHS Vaccination Centre opened at the arena on 25 January 2021, taking up the space previously occupied by the drive-through phlebotomy clinic before its relocation.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The vaccination centre remained open until 26 July, when it was relocated to the former [NHS Blood and Transplant](/wiki/NHS_Blood_and_Transplant "NHS Blood and Transplant") building next to the Northern General Hospital to allow the arena to reopen for its usual events.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In April 2020, the interior space of the arena was used as a storage location for 1,800 additional [hospital beds](/wiki/Hospital_bed "Hospital bed") which were being delivered to hospitals in Sheffield to support pandemic operations. Photographs of the hospital beds inside the arena later leaked onto social media, and the local NHS trust issued a statement denying rumours that the arena was going to be repurposed as a [Nightingale temporary hospital](/wiki/NHS_Nightingale_Hospitals "NHS Nightingale Hospitals").[[22]](#cite_note-22) In August 2020, an [ophthalmology](/wiki/Ophthalmology "Ophthalmology") clinic was opened inside the arena featuring eye imaging suites, remaining in operation until the arena reopened for normal usage after the pandemic.

## Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Events")]

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Slipknot_%40_Sheffield_Arena.jpg/250px-Slipknot_%40_Sheffield_Arena.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slipknot_@_Sheffield_Arena.jpg)

[Slipknot](/wiki/Slipknot_(band) "Slipknot (band)") @ Sheffield Arena, January 2020 – We Are Not Your Kind World Tour

Sheffield's own Arctic Monkeys broke the Arena ticket sales records when they instantly sold out over 13,600 tickets for their date at the arena in November 2013. The previous record of 13,300 was held by fellow Sheffield band Pulp in December 2012.[[9]](#cite_note-auto-9)

The venue's record attendance for a seated music concert is currently held by Little Mix when 12,485 customers attended their Get Weird Tour Saturday 23 April 2016.[[9]](#cite_note-auto-9) The record for most shows in Sheffield Arena is held by Irish boyband [Westlife](/wiki/Westlife "Westlife") with 32 shows played to date.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

British-Irish boy-band, [One Direction](/wiki/One_Direction "One Direction") played their last concert, before going on an indefinite hiatus, on 31 October 2015 at the arena. The arena held from somewhere between 35,000–37,000 people raising over 3 million dollars.

### Comedy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Comedy")]

The arena is a regular venue for some of the world's best known Comedians such as [Russell Brand](/wiki/Russell_Brand "Russell Brand"), [Lee Evans](/wiki/Lee_Evans_(comedian) "Lee Evans (comedian)"), [Jack Whitehall](/wiki/Jack_Whitehall "Jack Whitehall"), [Eddie Izzard](/wiki/Eddie_Izzard "Eddie Izzard") and [Peter Kay](/wiki/Peter_Kay "Peter Kay") who in 2011 played the arena for 5 nights running with all shows selling out within hours of going on sale. In June 2018 [Michael McInytyre](/wiki/Michael_McIntyre "Michael McIntyre") broke the venue's record attendance for a seated comedy show when his Big World Tour played the FlyDSA Arena on Saturday 23 June 2018 in front of 12,347 fans.

### Sheffield Steelers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sheffield Steelers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Sheffield_Steelers_v_Guildford_Flames_%40_Sheffield_Arena%2C_January_2020.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Steelers_v_Guildford_Flames_%40_Sheffield_Arena%2C_January_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Steelers_v_Guildford_Flames_@_Sheffield_Arena,_January_2020.jpg)

Sheffield Steelers v [Guildford Flames](/wiki/Guildford_Flames "Guildford Flames") @ Sheffield Arena, January 2020

The arena is the home to the city's [Elite Ice Hockey League](/wiki/Elite_Ice_Hockey_League "Elite Ice Hockey League") team, the [Sheffield Steelers](/wiki/Sheffield_Steelers "Sheffield Steelers"), who began to play at the arena in 1991. Whilst the standard capacity for ice hockey at the arena is around 9,300,[[5]](#cite_note-auto1-5) the Steelers have recorded crowds of over 10,000 in the building with extra seating being installed at the venue. In 2019 the Steelers were the highest ranked team in the UK in terms of attendance and 35th in Europe drawing an average crowd of 6,045 to the arena.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The first ever competitive Sheffield Steelers game at Sheffield Arena took place on Sunday 6 October 1991, when the Steelers were held to a 3–3 draw against [Chelmsford Chieftains](/wiki/Chelmsford_Chieftains "Chelmsford Chieftains") in front of 300 spectators. However, the first Ice Hockey game played at the Sheffield Arena took place on Thursday 26 September 1991 when [Durham Wasps](/wiki/Durham_Wasps "Durham Wasps") beat a Sheffield Steelers select 7–1 to win the World Leisure Cup. Durham's [Rick Brebant](/wiki/Rick_Brebant "Rick Brebant") scored the first goal at 2:18 in front of an estimated crowd of 2,000.

In February 2020 it was announced that the arena had secured planning permission to install a new 22-ton machine to make production of the ice surface more cost-effective and environmentally friendly.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Disney On Ice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Disney On Ice")]

[Disney On Ice](/wiki/Disney_On_Ice "Disney On Ice") holds the record for the most performances & customers of any touring show to perform at the Arena. Feld Entertainment has promoted the show every year since the building opened over November/December. As of 2013 there have been 260 performances seen by 1.3 million customers.[[9]](#cite_note-auto-9)

### Other events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other events")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Sheffield_Arena_Interior.jpg/250px-Sheffield_Arena_Interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield_Arena_Interior.jpg)

Sheffield Arena Interior

The arena was the location for the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)")'s 1992 [Sheffield Rally](/wiki/Sheffield_Rally "Sheffield Rally"). Attended by over 10,000 party members and the entire shadow cabinet. Held just before that year's general election, which Labour was widely expected to win, the event is often seen as a factor in the party's defeat.[[33]](#cite_note-BBC97-33) While the event was seen as a success to those within the hall, many commentators, and Labour politicians, viewed it as triumphalist in nature.[[33]](#cite_note-BBC97-33) However, it has also been asserted that the event had negligible impact on the outcome,[[34]](#cite_note-34) with a fall in the party's support having already occurred.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

TV shows such as *The X Factor*, *[Britain's Got Talent](/wiki/Britain%27s_Got_Talent "Britain's Got Talent")*, *[Strictly Come Dancing](/wiki/Strictly_Come_Dancing "Strictly Come Dancing")* and *[Dancing on Ice](/wiki/Dancing_on_Ice "Dancing on Ice")* visit the arena every year as part of their tours. It hosted the BBC revival of *[Gladiators](/wiki/Gladiators_(2024_British_TV_series) "Gladiators (2024 British TV series)")* in 2023[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) and will host it again on select dates during August 2024; the arena previously hosted the live show during Easter 1995.

The arena has hosted major special high-profile events, including The [International Indian Film Academy Awards](/wiki/International_Indian_Film_Academy_Awards "International Indian Film Academy Awards"), which took place on Saturday, 9 June 2007 and was broadcast live around the world. This included a season hosted by the city of [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield") and nearby [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds"). Also, in 2009, the arena hosted the annual [BBC Radio 1Xtra](/wiki/BBC_Radio_1Xtra "BBC Radio 1Xtra") concert and the 2009 [BBC Sports Personality of the Year](/wiki/BBC_Sports_Personality_of_the_Year "BBC Sports Personality of the Year").

The arena has hosted major boxing events such as when [Joe Calzaghe](/wiki/Joe_Calzaghe "Joe Calzaghe") first won his [WBO](/wiki/WBO "WBO") world title here on 11 October 1997 against former world champion [Chris Eubank](/wiki/Chris_Eubank "Chris Eubank"). On the same bill Sheffield born [Naseem Hamed](/wiki/Naseem_Hamed "Naseem Hamed") defended his WBO title against Jose Badillo. On 26 November 2005 [Ricky Hatton](/wiki/Ricky_Hatton "Ricky Hatton") extended his unbeaten record to 40–0, defending his [IBF](/wiki/IBF "IBF") and [Ring Magazine](/wiki/Ring_Magazine "Ring Magazine") Junior Welterweight world titles and winning the [WBA](/wiki/World_Boxing_Association "World Boxing Association") belt by 9th-round knockout against [Carlos Maussa](/wiki/Carlos_Maussa "Carlos Maussa"). [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield") fighter [Clinton Woods](/wiki/Clinton_Woods "Clinton Woods") also defended his IBF light-heavyweight title against Mexican [Julio César González](/wiki/Julio_C%C3%A9sar_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Julio César González") on two occasions, winning on points both times. [Kell Brook](/wiki/Kell_Brook "Kell Brook") has had many fights here, his most notable being a fourth-round knockout victory of [Vyacheslav Senchenko](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Senchenko "Vyacheslav Senchenko").

The [Great Britain men's national basketball team](/wiki/Great_Britain_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Great Britain men's national basketball team") played a test match against [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_basketball_team "Portugal national basketball team") in that arena in preparation for the 2012 Olympics.

As well as being home to the [Sheffield Steelers](/wiki/Sheffield_Steelers "Sheffield Steelers") the venue has also hosted other ice hockey events such as the 2019 Super Series between USA and Canada featuring many ex-[NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL") stars[[38]](#cite_note-38) and has acted as a neutral site for the [EIHL](/wiki/EIHL "EIHL") [Challenge Cup](/wiki/Challenge_Cup_(UK_ice_hockey) "Challenge Cup (UK ice hockey)") final

The arena has hosted many [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling") events held by companies including [WWE](/wiki/WWE "WWE"), [NXT](/wiki/NXT_(WWE_brand) "NXT (WWE brand)") and [5 Star Wrestling](/wiki/5_Star_Wrestling "5 Star Wrestling") over the years dating as far back as the [WWE](/wiki/WWE "WWE") [UK Rampage](/wiki/UK_Rampage "UK Rampage") events in [1992](/wiki/UK_Rampage_(1992) "UK Rampage (1992)") and [1993](/wiki/UK_Rampage_(1993) "UK Rampage (1993)")

Since 2007, it has played host to the [Premier League Darts](/wiki/Premier_League_Darts "Premier League Darts").

Every year students from both Sheffield Hallam and The University of Sheffield assemble to watch the final game of their varsity play out as both universities ice hockey teams play and the stadium fills to capacity.

## Management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Management")]

[ASM Global](/wiki/ASM_Global "ASM Global") has taken over the operation of [Utilita Arena Sheffield](/wiki/Utilita_Arena_Sheffield "Utilita Arena Sheffield") and [Sheffield City Hall](/wiki/Sheffield_City_Hall "Sheffield City Hall"), starting in January 2025. This marks a new chapter for both venues, with ASM Global aiming to reimagine the Utilita Arena as a world-class touring destination.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Road")]

The arena is easily accessible by road with the nearest motorway junctions being Junction 34 of the [M1](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway") if approaching from the North and Junction 33 of the M1 if approaching from the South. The arena has parking for around 1,200 cars[[40]](#cite_note-40) which is often available to pre-book in advance at a discounted rate.

### Tram

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Tram")]

The arena is accessible via the [South Yorkshire Supertram](/wiki/South_Yorkshire_Supertram "South Yorkshire Supertram") with the primary stop, [Arena / Olympic Legacy Park](/wiki/Sheffield_Supertram "Sheffield Supertram"), being served by both the Yellow Route and Tram Train. The [Valley Centertainment](/wiki/Valley_Centertainment "Valley Centertainment") entertainment complex nearby provides a secondary stop for the arena, Valley Centertainment, which is also served by the Yellow Route and Tram Train. This stop has Park & Ride facilities.

### Train

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Train")]

There are 3 train stations that are connected to the arena by Supertram – [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield_station "Sheffield station"), [Meadowhall Interchange](/wiki/Meadowhall_Interchange "Meadowhall Interchange") and [Rotherham Central](/wiki/Rotherham_Central_station "Rotherham Central station") – as well as nearby [Darnall](/wiki/Darnall_railway_station "Darnall railway station") which is a 5-minute drive from the arena.

### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Arena&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Bus")]

The arena is serviced by many bus routes (including [X1 Steel Link](/wiki/X1_Steel_Link "X1 Steel Link")) stopping at the Sheffield Arena and/or Arena Square stops nearby.
